narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48.7% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, without considering gender or race. The three biggest influences on your specific prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. Most notably, your strong performance in Year 3—landing you in the 8th decile, well above the typical 5.91 decile for those who passed the bar—pushed the model firmly toward expecting you to succeed, acting as your strongest positive signal. However, your LSAT score of 32, notably lower than the 37.27 average among bar passers, had the largest pull in the opposite direction, significantly raising the model's concern about failure risk. Your Year 1 decile of 4, below the 6.04 average for successful students, added a smaller but still concerning nudge toward predicting a struggle on the bar. Other aspects, like your upper-middle family income bracket aligning closely with many who passed and your full-time study plans matching the norm for admittees, played lesser roles and didn't sway things much. Compared to typical accepted students, your later academic turnaround stands out positively, but the earlier grades and LSAT created enough doubt to tip the scales. While this is disappointing, especially given your upward trajectory, it reflects a cautious approach to ensure we admit those with the highest predicted odds of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the 37+ range, as it carries heavy weight, and highlight your Year 3 improvement in any reapplication or appeals. Gathering stronger references or additional test prep evidence could also help demonstrate your potential. You're already showing resilience with that Year 3 jump—building on it strategically could change the outcome next time. Keep pushing; many students in similar spots have turned things around.
